topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rejection email sat glowing on the screen, a stark white rectangle against the muted grey of my desk. I stared at the words, the carefully crafted paragraphs about my potential dissolving into meaningless rejection. My hands, usually steady when writing essays, began to tremble slightly, the tremor running up my forearms. I slowly pushed the laptop away, the faint click of the lid closing sounding unnaturally loud in the quiet room. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain back just enough to see the bustling campus below, a vibrant tapestry of activity that felt impossibly distant. A heavy, dull ache settled in my chest, making each breath feel shallow. I traced the condensation on the glass with a fingertip, the chill seeping into my skin.

[story 2]

"No." The single word echoed in the empty hallway of my dorm room. I reread the notification three times, each repetition a fresh stab of disappointment. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to swallow. I slumped onto my bed, the mattress dipping under my weight with a sigh that felt too large for my frame. I picked up a worn paperback, but the words swam on the page, refusing to anchor my attention. My shoulders drooped, pulling my neck into a defeated curve. I let out a slow, shaky exhale, the air tasting thin and unsatisfying. I didn't move for a long time, just letting the weight of the news settle deep into my bones.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the interview in my mind, searching for a misstep, a moment where I had faltered. The memory brought a hot flush to my cheeks, quickly followed by a cold wave of emptiness. Every nerve ending felt exposed, hypersensitive to the silence of the room. When I finally looked at the screen again, the colors seemed muted, the text blurring slightly at the edges. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my room like a caged animal. My movements were jerky, uncoordinated. I stopped by the door, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame, and for a moment, I just leaned there, feeling utterly depleted, the energy drained out of me entirely.
